coredata.dc:description: In this study, a high-throughput point-of-care testing (HT-POCT) system for detecting serum iron was developed using a hydrophobic deep eutectic solvent (HDES) fluorescence detection platform. This machine learning-assisted portable platform enables intelligent and rapid detection of trace iron ions. Blue fluorescent hydrophobic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using the solvothermal method. The CQDs exhibit a notable quantum yield (QY) of 36.6%, demonstrating exceptional luminescent characteristics and precise, sensitive detection capabilities for Fe3+ ions. By incorporating CQDs into specially filtered HDESs, this blend serves a dual function of concentrating iron ions from the sample and facilitating their detection. The collaboration between the two enhances the fluorescence detection signal significantly, while reducing interference from hydrophilic substances. The limit of detection can be as low as 33 nM. The principles of synthesizing HDESs and the process of extracting Fe3+ using HDESs fluorescence detection system were modeled using density functional theory (DFT). As the concentration of Fe3+ increases, the fluorescence signal detected from the sample decreases, accompanied by visible color changes when exposed to ultraviolet light. The machine learning-assisted portable platform is designed to capture fluorescence images of samples directly. The application developed using the YOLOv8 algorithm efficiently analyzes multiple samples in single or multiple images, simultaneously extracting color data from each sample and determining the concentration of iron ions. The Relative Standard Deviations (RSDs) for both single-sample and multi-sample tests were less than 10%. The machine learning-assisted portable platform provides a reliable method for detecting trace iron ions.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a high-throughput point-of-care testing (HT-POCT) system for detecting serum iron was developed using a hydrophobic deep eutectic solvent (HDES) fluorescence detection platform. This machine learning-assisted portable platform enables intelligent and rapid detection of trace iron ions. Blue fluorescent hydrophobic carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized using the solvothermal method. The CQDs exhibit a notable quantum yield (QY) of 36.6%, demonstrating exceptional luminescent characteristics and precise, sensitive detection capabilities for Fe3+ ions. By incorporating CQDs into specially filtered HDESs, this blend serves a dual function of concentrating iron ions from the sample and facilitating their detection. The collaboration between the two enhances the fluorescence detection signal significantly, while reducing interference from hydrophilic substances. The limit of detection can be as low as 33 nM. The principles of synthesizing HDESs and the process of extracting Fe3+ using HDESs fluorescence detection system were modeled using density functional theory (DFT). As the concentration of Fe3+ increases, the fluorescence signal detected from the sample decreases, accompanied by visible color changes when exposed to ultraviolet light. The machine learning-assisted portable platform is designed to capture fluorescence images of samples directly. The application developed using the YOLOv8 algorithm efficiently analyzes multiple samples in single or multiple images, simultaneously extracting color data from each sample and determining the concentration of iron ions. The Relative Standard Deviations (RSDs) for both single-sample and multi-sample tests were less than 10%. The machine learning-assisted portable platform provides a reliable method for detecting trace iron ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron ions play a vital role in biological systems, facilitating the production of enzymes that are involved in electron transfer, oxygen uptake, and metabolic processes [1,2]. Serum iron level variations can indicate conditions like anemia, infections, and tumors [3,4]. Traditional iron analysis methods like ICP-MS and FIA, while sensitive, require costly equipment, skilled personnel, and complex procedures. In contrast, point-of-care testing (POCT) offers on-site analysis, reducing time, cost, and technical demands [5,6]. Recently, there's been a shift towards developing rapid, portable, and high-throughput (HT) sensors, with HT-POCT emerging as a key trend in future detection methods.

Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs) are a novel class of zero-dimensional carbon-based nanomaterials with wide UV absorption, precise fluorescence emission, high quantum yield, and resistance to photobleaching [7]. These properties have led to their extensive use in photocatalysis, bio-imaging, and compound detection [8–10]. For example, Umar et al. [11] synthesized N-CQDs using citric acid and ethylenediamine, demonstrating effective fluorescence quenching by Fe3+, enabling trace Fe3+ detection. Similarly, Liu et al. [12] prepared N-CQDs with citric acid and urea, showing a linear fluorescence response to Cu2+ concentrations between 0.3–0.7 μM, with a detection limit of 0.071 μM. CQDs thus hold significant potential as fluorescent probes for HT-POCT development [13]. However, their practical application is limited by interference resistance and detection thresholds. In 2015, Kroon et al. [14,15] introduced hydrophobic deep eutectic solvents (HDESs), a novel DES subclass for metal ion leaching, extraction, and purification [16–19]. HDESs enable metal recovery without the need for additional reducing agents or costly solvents [20]. For example, Pius et al. [21] achieved 99 % efficiency in extracting Fe3+ and Mn2+ using a HDES composed of lidocaine and decanoic acid, while Tereshatov et al. [22] effectively extracted indium with an HDES made of decanoic acid, oleic acid, and ibuprofen. DESs are cost-effective, biodegradable, and reusable. This study demonstrates that combining CQDs with HDESs significantly enhances detection sensitivity and minimizes interference from hydrophilic substances.

Smartphones have rapidly evolved from communication tools to multifunctional devices with advanced digital imaging, making them ideal platforms for sensor development and on-site testing in POCT across biomedicine, environmental monitoring, and food safety [13,23,24]. Machine learning (ML) methods, particularly Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), excel in image and speech recognition, natural language processing, and biomedicine, and are widely used in various sectors. The YOLO algorithm, a one-stage object detection method, processes smartphone-captured images by dividing them into grids, with each grid cell serving as the center for anchor boxes input to CNNs [25]. CNNs streamline image processing by eliminating complex feature extraction and reconstruction, utilizing layers like convolutional, pooling, and fully connected layers. For instance, Lu et al. [25] developed a system using YOLOv3 and smartphones for rapid tetracycline antibiotics detection via fluorescence sensing, enabling fast and reliable concentration analysis.

In this study, hydrophobic CQDs prepared have excellent luminescence characteristics, and have significant specificity and sensitivity to Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 1 , combining CQDs with HDESs enhances target compound concentration and enables simultaneous detection, significantly improving CQDs detectability while reducing interference from hydrophilic substances. The synthesis principle of HDESs and the process of extracting Fe3+ using HDESs fluorescence detection system were modeled through density functional theory (DFT) simulations. As the concentration of Fe3+ increases, the fluorescence signal detected from the sample decreases, and visible color changes occur when exposed to ultraviolet light. The machine learning-assisted portable platform can capture fluorescence images of samples directly. The application developed using the YOLOv8 algorithm efficiently analyzes multiple samples in single or multiple images, simultaneously extracting color data from each sample and determining the concentration of iron ions. The machine learning-assisted portable platform provides a reliable solution for high-throughput point-of-care testing (HT-POCT) of trace amounts of iron ions. This study holds significant theoretical and research value for the application of HT-POCT in the detection of various compounds, further expanding the potential applications of quantum dots and deep eutectic solvents in the field of detection.

The reagents used in this study included choline chloride, urea, cinnamyl alcohol, quinine sulfate, trioctyl phosphorus oxide, tetradecanol, menthol, camphor, thymol, capric acid, lauric acid, citral, and lactic acid. All ionized salt solutions were prepared in deionized water. The metal salts included FeCl3, FeCl2, ZnCl2, CaCl2, MgCl2, AgCl, HgCl2, KCl, CuCl2, NaCl, CaCO3, NaBr, NaHCO3, Na2SO3, NaI, Cu(NO3)2, NaCOOH, NH3NO3, L-cysteine, L-glutamic acid, L-tyrosine, L-tryptophan, L-arginine, L-Proline, L-Lysine, L-Glycine. The image acquisition device is an iPhone 12.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and selectivity determination of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The specific preparation and characterization process of quantum dots are in the supplementary materials [26,27].

Experiments were conducted with decanoic acid, trioctyl phosphine oxide, muscimol, tetradecanol, citral, lidocaine, and eugenol as hydrogen bond acceptors (HBAs), and with tetradecanol, cinnamyl alcohol, camphor, menthol, and caprylic acid as hydrogen bond donors (HBDs). The preparation method of HDESs is the same as described above.

HDESs were evaluated based on their hydrophobic properties, the stability of HDESs fluorescence detection system, and their ability to extract Fe3+. The HDESs and deionized water were mixed in a centrifuge tube and vortexed. If the liquids separated and the DESs appeared as clear liquids, they were classified as HDESs and moved on to the next screening step. Following the integration of HDESs with CQDs, it is essential that the fluorescence of the CQDs remains stable. The Fe3+ extraction capability of HDESs fluorescence detection system was then tested by mixing of the prepared solution with various concentrations and volumes of Fe3+ ions, vortexing, and centrifuging. The residual Fe3+ in the solution was measured using ICP-MS to determine the extraction efficiency of the HDESs.

The investigation of HDESs synthesis and metal extraction was conducted using NMR spectroscopy and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. Theoretical computations and simulations of the processes involved in HDES synthesis and metal extraction were conducted using the Gaussian software the B3LYP/6-31G(d) level, which operates based on density functional theory (DFT). The molecular electrostatic surface potential (ESP) of HDESs was determined using Multiwfn 3.7 and VMD software [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of HDESs fluorescence detection system
Characterization of HDESs fluorescence detection system
ce:section.ce:para: The characterization method of HDES fluorescence detection system is consistent with that of CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test of Fe3+ by HDESs fluorescence detection system
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs nanomaterials were dispersed in the HDES solution, vortexed, and centrifuged for integration. A mixture of HDESs fluorescence detection system and 10, 20 and 30 mL of Fe3+ solution at varying concentrations was vortexed and centrifuged. The fluorescence intensity of the supernatant was measured. The relationship between I0-I/I0 and the Fe3+ concentration was linearly evaluated to determine the optimal volume ratio. At the optimal material-liquid ratio, a mixture of 1 mL of HDESs fluorescence detection system solution and Fe3+ ion solution at pH levels of 2, 5 and 7 was vortexed and centrifuged. The resulting supernatant was collected, and the fluorescence intensity of the supernatant was measured. This data was then analyzed to determine the optimal pH. HDESs fluorescence detection system was developed.

Samples in the cuvettes were exposed to an ultraviolet light with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The UV light was fixed on the top of an enclosed box, and the light might have reflected off the surface of the inner walls of the box, which would have influenced the results. To eliminate this interference, a totally black background was employed inside the box. Subsequently, fluorescence images of these cuvettes with samples were photographed at multiple distances utilizing an iPhone 12.

The cuvette with sample and the solution of Fe3+ were classified into two categories according to their different outline features, defined as ‘cuvette’ and ‘liquid’, respectively. The dataset consisted of 72 images, with 324 ‘cuvette’ and 324 ‘liquid’, which were manually labeled in the Roboflow software, reaching a total count of 648 annotated objects, and no augmentations were performed. Each image was labeled with the class number of the object and the coordinates of the edges of the associated bounding box. The whole data set was split into a training set (71%), a validation set (19%), and a testing set (10%), where the cuvettes varied in number, color, and distance from the camera, aiming to enhance the accuracy of object detection.

A lightweight model, YOLOv8n, proposed by Ultralytics [29], was pre-trained on the COCO dataset and subsequently fine-tuned on the dataset containing two kinds of objects, ‘cuvette’ and ‘liquid’, to optimize its hyper-parameters and learn more abstract feature representations. The input images of the model were tuned to 640 × 640 pixels and the model was trained utilizing a batch size of 16 on a single 16 GB NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4070 Ti SUPER GPU, which effectively boosted the processing speed. The Windows 10 system integrated with Python 3.12, PyTorch 2.3.0, CUDA 12.1, and cuDNN v8.8.1 ran on the computer with an Intel® Core™ i9-10900 K CPU and a 128 GB sized main memory. To better visualize the results, the camera module, YOLOv8 algorithm, and display units were integrated to form a self-designed graphical user interface (GUI) application named “HT-Detector,” which was developed by utilizing the PySide6 library created by the Qt Company to achieve a machine learning-assisted HT-POCT integrated portable platform based on HDESs-CQDs as shown in Fig. 8.

The study investigated the levels of Fe3+ in rat serum, and tap water. Before analysis, the collected samples underwent specific pre-treatment procedures. Rat blood samples were subjected to centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 20 min. The resulting serum was diluted 50-fold with PBS, adjusted to a pH of 7.0, and stored at 4 °C. Tap water was initially filtered to eliminate large aggregates, followed by filtration through a 0.22 µm cellulose membrane to remove suspended solids. The pH was adjusted to 7.0, and the treated water was stored accordingly. To validate the accuracy of our developed detection method, the concentration of iron ions in the original samples was measured using inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). Subsequently, the iron ion concentrations in the prepared spiked samples were assessed at room temperature using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. A machine learning detection platform was utilized to determine the iron ion concentrations in both single and multiple spiked samples. To ensure the accuracy and reliability of the detection results, each experiment was performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and chemical characteristics of CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The physical and chemical properties of quantum dots are essential factors that influence their behavior. This research focused on a detailed analysis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using advanced techniques such as High-Resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), X-ray mapping, and Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDS), as illustrated in Fig. 2 . The HRTEM image in Fig. 2a clearly illustrates that the synthesized CQDs possess a spherical shape, are uniformly dispersed without clumping, and can be evenly distributed in a solution, facilitating their recognition and attachment to specific targets. Additionally, the enlarged HRTEM image in Fig. 2b displays a single CQD with a well-defined lattice structure, showing a lattice spacing of approximately 0.20 nm. The CQDs exhibit diameters ranging from 1.75 to 4.25 nm, with an average particle size of 2.6 nm, as indicated in Fig. 2d. These dimensions fall within the recommended size range of 10 nm for carbon quantum dots, confirming the successful production of carbon nano-quantum dots in this investigation.

In order to confirm the effective integration of DESs into CQDs, an analysis was conducted to determine the types and proportions of elements present in the CQDs. This analysis utilized X-ray mapping (as depicted in Fig. 2c) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) (as shown in Fig. 2e). The primary elements observed, including carbon (C), oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), and chlorine (Cl), were found to be uniformly distributed on the surface of the CQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 2c. Specifically, carbon was predominantly sourced from cinnamyl alcohol, while nitrogen and chlorine originated from the DES. The respective percentage compositions of these elements are detailed in Table S1. Ultimately, the successful integration of DESs into CQDs was conclusively verified. Additionally, X-ray diffraction (XRD) data presented in Fig. 2f indicated distinct peaks at 2θ values of 15.5°, 17.2°, 22.0°, and 28.3°, which were attributed to the diffraction peaks of a novel crystalline phase resulting from the introduction of DES. These findings underscore the crystalline nature of quantum dots.

The optical characteristics of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were assessed using UV–visible spectrophotometry and fluorescence spectrophotometry. The CQDs solution showed a slight yellow hue, and when exposed to UV light, it emitted a vivid blue fluorescence (refer to Fig. 2g, h). The absorption (Abs), excitation (Ex), and emission (Em) spectra of the synthesized quantum dots are depicted in Fig. 2j. The UV absorption spectrum exhibited two distinct absorption peaks at 210 nm and 250 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transitions of the CC bond and the aromatic sp2 system, respectively. The presence of a conjugated system contributed to the strong fluorescence emission of the quantum dots. Additionally, the p-π conjugation between the lone electron pair in −C-OH and CC induces a chromogenic effect, enhancing photoluminescence [30,31]. As shown in Fig. 2k and l, the optimal excitation and emission wavelengths for the CQDs were determined to be 250 nm and 308 nm, respectively. The excitation and emission spectra of the CQDs exhibited minimal overlap, ensuring low interference and high accuracy in heavy metal detection, attributed to a significant Stokes shift. The quantum yield (QY) of the experimentally produced CQDs was measured at 36.6 %, surpassing the values reported for most quantum dots in the existing literature.

When quantum dots (CQDs) absorb sufficient energy, their electrons transition to higher energy levels, creating excited states. The fundamental concept behind fluorescence emission involves the release of photons as electrons return from the excited state to the ground state. In this process, electrons are transferred from the enriched region to PNJ holes when a photoelectron jumps (Fig. 2i). Consequently, the development of intermolecularly coupled PNJ structures proves to be an efficient method for enhancing photoluminescence. The utilization of a p-p conjugated structure is advantageous for PNJ construction because photoelectrons can transition to the unsaturated region of the p orbital. Compounds containing a carbonyl group or heteroatoms, such as nitrogen (N), can form a p-p conjugated structure [32,33]. Meanwhile, nitrogen atoms typically form strong covalent bonds (such as NH or NC bonds), while carbonyl groups (CO) also form strong double bonds. These strong bonds restrict the free rotation of the molecule, increasing the overall structural rigidity. The rigid planar structure effectively reduces twisting and bending within the molecule, thereby providing a clearer overlap of electron orbitals. This efficient orbital overlap usually enhances electron mobility and extends the lifetime of excited states, which facilitates electronic transitions. As shown in Figure S2, the Fourier transform infrared spectra of CQDs before and after the introduction of DESs. The figure shows that the quantum dots modified with deep eutectic solvents (DESs) exhibit significant absorption peaks at 3300–3500 cm−1, 1500–1650 cm−1, and 1650–1750 cm−1, which correspond to the infrared characteristic peaks of NH and carbonyl groups. This indicates that the DESs-modified carbon quantum dots (CQDs) contain a rich abundance of nitrogen atoms and carbonyl groups. In the context of this research, the CQDs modified by DESs exhibit enhanced photoluminescence properties [33].

Cinnamaldehyde was chemically linked with urea through a dehydration process, and the potential for light-induced luminescence of the synthesized quantum dots was investigated using computational modeling. The luminescent characteristics are intricately linked to the fundamental state (S0), excited state (S1), and excited triplet state (T1) of the quantum dots. The optimal configurations of the quantum dots in the S0, S1, and T1 states, as depicted in Fig. 3 a, exhibit structurally rigid and planar arrangements that facilitate electronic transitions [12]. Fig. 3b illustrates the distribution of Molecular Electrostatic Potential Surfaces (ESP) of CQDs in various electronic states (S0, S1, and T1). In this representation, areas in red signify positive ESP values, while those in blue indicate negative ESP values. The negative electron spin polarization (ESP) values observed in CQDs enhance the adsorption of metal ions. Furthermore, using density functional theory, the energies of the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) of CQDs in the mentioned states were calculated (Fig. 3c). The HOMO-LUMO gap (ΔE = ELUMO − EHOMO) is a critical parameter that reflects the optical characteristics of the system. A lower LUMO energy suggests enhanced electron acceptance capability, while a higher HOMO energy indicates superior electron donation ability. The difference in energy levels between HOMO and LUMO serves as an indicator of compound stability, with smaller energy gaps correlating with increased stability [34]. The energy gaps (ΔE) between the LUMO and HOMO for S0, S1, and T1 were found to be 0.18949 H, 0.08049 H, and 0.18630 H, respectively, consistent with the stability order of S0 > T1 > S1.

The remarkable detection capabilities of carbon quantum dot fluorescence sensors are attributed to their excellent fluorescence stability. In this study, an investigation was conducted on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs under various conditions. The results depicted in Fig. 4 a show that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs remains stable across a wide pH range of 1 to 12. Furthermore, exposure to sunlight (Fig. 4b) and 3 h of UV irradiation (figure S3) did not lead to a significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity of CQDs, indicating their high resistance to photobleaching. Unlike traditional organic dyes or fluorophores, which often exhibit noticeable photobleaching under light excitation within a few minutes, CQDs maintain their fluorescence intensity because of their stable chemical structure and the presence of surface functional groups such as hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amino groups. Additionally, minimal changes in the fluorescence intensity of CQDs were observed within the temperature range of 0 to 75℃ (Fig. 4c). These findings collectively underscore the exceptional fluorescence stability of CQDs, establishing a strong foundation for their practical applications.

For fluorescence probes, selectivity is a critical criterion for evaluating their performance and is a prerequisite for detecting target substances in real-world samples. To assess the selectivity of the constructed fluorescence detection platform, we tested the fluorescence response of CQDs to various metal ions and amino acids. As shown in the inset of Fig. 4d and 4e, photographs of CQDs after the introduction of interfering ions under UV light illumination indicated that only the fluorescence of CQDs with added Fe3+ was quenched. Fig. 4d, 4e and S4 present bar graphs showing the relative fluorescence intensity I0-I/I0. The results demonstrate that only Fe3+ significantly quenched the fluorescence intensity of CQDs. Using CQDs as fluorescence probes under 250 nm excitation, fluorescence intensity measurements were conducted in the presence of varying concentrations of iron ions, enabling quantitative analysis of Fe3+ based on the relative fluorescence intensity I0-I/I0. As illustrated in Fig. 4f and Fig. 4g, when the quantum dots were present in alcohol, the quantitative data I0-I/I0 exhibited a good linear relationship with Fe3+ concentration in the range of 1 to 30 μM (R2 = 0.9953), with a detection limit of 0.35 μM.

In general, quenching mechanisms can be categorized into two main types: static quenching and dynamic quenching. Static quenching occurs when the quencher forms a non-luminescent complex with the fluorophore in its ground state. This process is characterized by the formation of stable complexes that inhibit fluorescence by preventing the emission of light. In contrast, dynamic quenching involves the interaction between the quencher and the excited-state fluorophore. During this interaction, energy or charge transfer can occur, leading to the non-radiative deactivation of the excited state. This means that the quencher effectively reduces the fluorescence intensity through transient interactions, which depend on the diffusion of the molecules in the solution [11,35]. Therefore, to determine the quenching mechanism between CQDs and Fe3+, it is essential to identify the fundamental mode of fluorescence quenching of Fe3+ on CQDs.

The fluorescence lifetimes of CQDs exhibited minimal change before and after the introduction of Fe3+, with τ0 = 7.3614 ns for CQDs and τ1 = 7.2460 ns for CQDs@Fe3+ (Fig. 4i). This suggests that Fe3+ acts as a quencher without affecting the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs in the excited state. Additionally, a linear correlation was observed between (I0/I-1) and Fe3+ concentration within the range of 0 to 30 μM (I0/I-1 = 0.0206[Fe3+] − 0.0552). This relationship allows for the determination of the bimolecular quenching constant, Kq, based on the fluorescence lifetime τ0 and the Stern-Volmer equation (Kq*τ = KSV). The calculated value of Kq, approximately falling within the range of 2.80*1012 M−1 s−1, significantly surpasses the maximum collisional quenching constant of 1010. This indicates that Fe3+ selectively quenches the fluorescence of CQDs through a static quenching mechanism, resulting in the formation of a stable complex between Fe3+ and CQDs. Compared to isolated carbon quantum dots (CQDs), the Zeta potential of CQDs@Fe3+ increases, suggesting that Fe3+ interacts with the coordinating groups on the surface of the quantum dots. This coordination shields the positive charge on the surface of the quantum dots (Fig. 4h), further confirming the formation of a complex between Fe3+ and CQDs [35].

As shown in Table 1 , a total of 24 types of HDESs were successfully synthesized. After vortex mixing and centrifugation with deionized water, they formed two distinct liquid layers. The upper layer was a clear solution of HDESs (Fig. 5 a). Among them, nine types of HDESs were capable of binding with CQDs, resulting in no crystallization and exhibiting fluorescence stability in HDESs fluorescence detection system complexes (Fig. 5b). After an initial evaluation, it was determined that HDESs consisting of a combination of trioctylphosphine oxide and tetradecanol in a 1:1 ratio, as well as capric acid and lauric acid in a 1:1 ratio, demonstrated efficient extraction of Fe3+ ions subsequent to their interaction with CQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 6 a and Fig. 6b. Analysis using ICP-MS indicated that the extraction efficiency of iron by the two specified types of HDESs exceeded 87 % across various initial concentrations (1, 10, 30 μM) and extraction volume ratios (1:10, 1:20 and 1:30), as show in Table 2 . As shown in figure S5, when CQDs were dried and dissolved in HDESs, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs remained almost unchanged at the same concentration. This suggests that the drying process and HDESs had minimal impact on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs.

Taking trioctylphosphine oxide: tetradecanol (1:1) as an example, we elucidated the formation mechanism of HDES using 1H NMR and FT-IR characterization methods. As shown in Fig. 5c, compared to the individual components, no additional new peaks were observed in the proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR) spectrum of the newly synthesized HDES. This indicates that no reaction occurred between the two components during preparation. Upon the conversion of MA into HDES, the hydroxyl peak of MA shifted from 1.59 ppm to 1.26 ppm (marked by red lines), indicating a strong hydrogen bonding interaction between MA and TOPO. After the formation of hydrogen bonds, the shielding effect of active hydrogen atoms typically weakens, resulting in an increase in the chemical shift of active hydrogen. Therefore, as the number of hydrogen bonds formed increases, the chemical shift of active hydrogen also increases, while it decreases conversely. Before forming hydrogen bonds with TOPO, MA molecules had already formed a certain number of hydrogen bonds among themselves. After forming hydrogen bonds with TOPO, the total number of hydrogen bonds formed decreases due to the larger steric hindrance of TOPO compared to MA. This results in a decrease in the chemical shift of MA's active hydrogen. As depicted in Fig. 5d, upon converting MA and TOPO into HDES, the hydroxyl peak (−OH) of MA in the infrared spectrum shifted from 3341.93 cm−1 to 355.11 cm−1, while the carbonyl peak (−CO) of TOPO shifted from 1636.89 cm−1 to 1626.12 cm−1. This indicates the formation of hydrogen bonds between the − OH group of MA and the − CO group of TOPO [36].

ESP arises from the interaction between atomic nuclei and electrons on the surface of a molecule [37]. ESP maps of MA and TOPO were generated using Multiwfn 3.7 and VMD. As depicted in Fig. 5e, the highest positive point in MA's ESP is near the H of − OH (43.29 kcal/mol). This suggests that the –OH group of MA is highly reactive and can initiate either electrophilic or nucleophilic reactions [38–41]. The most negative point in TOPO's ESP is near the O of − CO (−55.54 kcal/mol). In summary, MA acts as the hydrogen bond donor (HBD), TOPO as the hydrogen bond acceptor (HBA), and hydrogen bonding occurs between the hydrogen of MA's − OH and the carbonyl group of TOPO. figure S6 illustrate the ESP maps of synthesis of HDES consisting of capric acid and lauric acid.

Observations from FT-IR spectra of MA and TOPO before and after Fe3+ extraction (Fig. 6d) revealed that the carbonyl (−CO) peak of TOPO shifted from 1638.80 cm−1 to 1623.88 cm−1, while the hydroxyl (−OH) peak of MA shifted from 3336.57 cm−1 to 3451.49 cm−1. These shifts indicate that TOPO utilizes the − CO group and MA utilizes the − OH group for Fe3+ extraction. As depicted in Fig. 6c, upon binding with Fe3+, the signals of hydroxyl and adjacent methylene hydrogens in HDES were shielded, resulting in weakened signal intensities. ESP maps of MA and TOPO were generated using Multiwfn 3.7 and VMD (Fig. 6e). The maps revealed that the O surface of the − CO group in TOPO and the − OH surface in MA represent the most negative ESP points, making them active sites for electrophilic attack. The iron salt used in the experiment was ferric chloride, and the form of Fe3+ in water was FeCl4 −. DFT calculations demonstrated that MA and TOPO can combine with H+ in water through O (blue region) to form MA-H+ and TOPO-H+. Subsequently, due to the presence of larger positive regions (red regions) on MA-H + and TOPO-H+, they attract FeCl4 − to form complexes, thereby facilitating extraction. Both DFT calculations and FT-IR analysis results indicate that MA and TOPO can bind with Fe3+ to facilitate extraction processes [42–44]. Figure S7 illustrates the ESP maps of Fe3+ extraction using HDESs composed of capric acid and lauric acid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of HDESs fluorescence detection system
Characteristics of HDESs fluorescence detection system
ce:section.ce:para:
The HRTEM image depicted in Fig. 7
a illustrates that the morphology of CQDs remains unaltered following their interaction with HDESs. The CQDs retain their spherical shape, exhibiting uniform dispersion without any signs of aggregation. The enlarged high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image shown in Fig. 7b illustrates the surface characteristics of HDESs fluorescence detection system displaying distinct lattice patterns with a measured lattice spacing of approximately 0.19 nm. In addition, the diameters of the particles ranged from 1.51 to 4.97 nm, with a mean diameter of 2.75 nm as illustrated in Fig. 7c.

In comparison to CQDs, HDESs Fluorescence Detection System exhibit higher stability at elevated temperatures, with negligible changes in fluorescence intensity observed within the range of 0–85 °C (Fig. 7f). This phenomenon can be attributed to the increased viscosity of HDESs fluorescence detection system and the diminished energy dissipation resulting from particle interactions. The surface of quantum dots is rich in reactive functional groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. When combined with hydrophobic deep eutectic solvents, which are also abundant in active groups, these functional groups interact through hydrogen bonding, forming an extensive hydrogen bond network. This large network enhances the structural stability and functional capabilities of the system, facilitating improved interactions with target analytes, which can be advantageous in various applications such as sensing, catalysis, and separation processes. Moreover, following exposure to sunlight (as shown in Fig. 7d) and UV radiation for a duration of 3 h (as depicted in Fig. 7e), there was no significant reduction in the fluorescence intensity of HDESs fluorescence detection system. Fig. 7g, 7 h and S8 illustrate bar graphs presenting relative fluorescence intensity data (I0-I/I0). The findings validated the high selectivity of HDESs fluorescence detection system toward Fe3+ ions. Through the integration of fluorescence lifetime (τ) measurements, it has been illustrated that the selective quenching of HDESs fluorescence detection system fluorescence by Fe3+ follows a static quenching mechanism (refer to Fig. 7i).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test of Fe3+ by HDESs fluorescence detection system
ce:section.ce:para: Solution of HDESs fluorescence detection system was combined with separate 10 mL and 20 mL Fe3+ aqueous solutions at a pH of 7. Centrifugation was performed to isolate the supernatant for further analysis. It was observed that within the concentration range of 0–30 μM, a strong linear correlation existed between the quantitative parameter I0-I/I0 and the concentration of Fe3+ (R2 ≥ 0.99). When the volume ratio is 1:20, the slope of the standard curve is 3.8 times greater than that at a volume ratio of 1:10. Conversely, at a volume ratio of 1:30, the detection limit of the system is reached. This suggests that the sensitivity of detection is higher at the 1:20 vol ratio (Fig. 7j). Fig. 7k illustrates the direct correlation between the quantitative data I0-I/I0 and Fe3+ concentration across different pH levels at a volumetric ratio of 1:20. Notably, at a pH of 7, an R2 value of 0.9964 was achieved, indicating that the most effective detection pH is 7. Under a volumetric ratio of 1:20 and at a pH of 7, a strong linear correlation was observed between the quantitative data I0-I/I0 and the concentration of Fe3+ within the range of 0.1 to 30 μM. The correlation coefficients (R2) were determined to be 0.9968 and 0.9963, as illustrated in Fig. 7l, m, n and o. The Limit of Detection is 33 nM. The presence of Fe3+ was effectively identified through the integration of HDESs with CQDs, resulting in significant reductions in quantitative and detection limits. The recovery rates detected in practical samples varied between 96.30% and 100.41%, as detailed in Table 3 .

As shown in Fig. 8f, the YOLOv8 model consists of three main parts: the backbone, neck, and head. The backbone extracted multiple features from fluorescence images with three channels: red, green, and blue. The neck utilized multi-scale feature fusion to combine the above features to ensure accurate predictions for images of various sizes. YOLOv8 employed a decoupled head to generate the annotated image. More details on these three parts and extended modules of YOLOv8 are illustrated in Fig. 8b and figure S9, respectively.

To showcase the effectiveness of the fine-tuned YOLOv8n on the fluorescence image dataset, the precision-recall (PR) curve and F1-score were applied to evaluate this model. They are calculated according to the following equations:
(4)
P r e c i s i o n =
T P
T P + F P
(5)
R e c a l l =
T P
T P + F N
where TP, FP, and FN stand for the number of True Positives, False Positives, and False Negatives, respectively.

Precision and recall are inversely related, in that if the number of false positives increases, then the number of false negatives often decreases, and vice versa. They can be combined to give a single measure, the F1 measure, which can be written in terms of precision and recall as:
(6)
F 1 =
2 × p r e c i s i o n × r e c a l l
p r e c i s i o n + r e c a l l
=
2 × T P
2 × T P + F N + F P
Mean average precision (mAP) is one of the most commonly used evaluation metrics for object detection and takes the average precision (AP) over all classes.

The original YOLOv8n with default hyper-parameters was fine-tuned, validated, and evaluated on the custom data set for 300 epochs, after which the training losses, including bounding box and class loss, and the mAP metrics were illustrated in Fig. 8c and 8d, respectively. All the other evaluation metrics are illustrated in figure S10-12. Combined with the final detection result shown in Fig. 8h, it concluded that the fine-tuned model YOLOv8n could successfully classify all the objects presented. The region of interest (ROI) for each sample was defined as a smaller area within the ‘liquid’ object, as shown in Fig. 8h. The bounding box of the ROI was proportional to the bounding box of the ‘liquid’ object. An image processing algorithm was performed to extract RGB values in the ROI and calculate the average RGB values. Additionally, multiple samples detected by the YOLOv8 algorithm would automatically be labeled with sequential numbers as their identifications (IDs).

Fig. 8a depicts the flowchart of the machine learning-assisted high-throughput detection of the concentration of Fe3+, which includes two stages: linear regression and concentration identification. Each sample has a known concentration in the linear regression phase, and all the samples are arranged in a single image. The HT-Detector application, which is integrated with YOLOv8, autonomously identifies 'liquid' objects within an image. It computes the average RGB values for the ROI and establishes a linear regression equation correlating the averaged RGB values with their respective concentrations, as illustrated in Fig. 8e. As the concentration of iron ions increases, the RGB values of the sample's fluorescence images all decrease. Notably, the Blue value shows a more significant reduction with increasing iron ion concentration, resulting in greater accuracy for real sample detection. Therefore, in this study, the Blue value is utilized as a reference for detection. Figure S13 shows the standard curve used during the detection of real samples. Furthermore, the application annotates the image with identifiers, concentrations, and RGB values beneath the corresponding cuvettes. It also presents the linear regression curve and formula alongside the annotated image.

In the concentration identification phase, a single or batch of fluorescence images containing samples with unknown concentrations are photographed by the camera attached to the enclosed box. The application autonomously processes these images to detect the ‘liquid’ objects, calculates the average RGB values in the ROI, determines the concentrations using the previously established linear regression formula, annotates the image with the IDs, concentrations, and RGB values beneath the corresponding cuvettes, and displays the scatter chart of RGB values vs. concentrations and the annotated image, as well as the detected RGB values and corresponding concentrations. The GUI application displaying the aforementioned information is shown in Fig. 8g, which illustrates the interface of the developed app named HT-Detector. As shown in the figure, the interface displays images of the real samples before and after software processing, a table of detection results for the real samples, the standard curve, images of the standard curve samples, and the standard curve table.

Owing to the platform, concentrations of Fe3+ of all samples in a single fluorescence image with a high resolution of 4032 × 3024 pixels, as well as the IDs and averaged RGB values, can be identified and annotated in the image within 335 ms, which is remarkably efficient compared to the conventional methods and yet maintains adequate accuracy 99.16 %, at the meanwhile, the accuracy of the single sample detection is 86.73 % (Table 4 ). Moreover, it can process dozens of fluorescence images of samples at the same time and then output the concentration of the target. All results will be presented in tabular and graphical formats. As shown in Fig. 8h, the fluorescence images and processed results of the samples used to generate the standard curve are presented, with the standard curve illustrated in Figure S13. This study utilized a machine learning detection platform to assess the recovery rates of spiked samples in serum and tap water. We set four standard concentrations of 10, 40, 60 and 100 respectively. The detection results are summarized in Table 4. The relative standard deviations (RSDs) for both single sample tests and simultaneous tests of 4 and 8 samples were all less than 10 %, indicating that the detection results from the machine learning platform are precise. The results of real sample detection are simply displayed in Fig. 8i-k, with all detection result images presented in Figures S15-17.
The codes, dataset, fine-tuned model, and detection results are available online at https://github.com/beefly-repo/HT-Detector.

This study compares previously reported fluorescence probe-based detection techniques. As shown in Table 5 , the synthesized CQDs has significant advantages over other methods in terms of required synthesis time and linear range, and the extremely low detection limit proves the stability and sensitivity of this method in the detection field. The portable detection platform developed in this study offers several advantages over methods reported in the literature. It eliminates the need for expensive and complex instruments, allows for on-site detection, and requires minimal technical expertise, making it both simple and user-friendly [45–49]. Unlike other studies that rely on professional third-party applications to manually collect color data [50–52,54–56,59], this study has developed a GUI application that can automatically detect objects, precisely extract color information, and rapidly calculate concentrations with the assistance of an efficient machine learning algorithm, YOLOv8. In comparison to YOLOv3 and YOLOv5, YOLOv8 introduces the C2f block for efficient feature extraction and fusion, enhancing the detection of multi-scale and multi-resolution targets. This improvement results in more sensitive and accurate detections. This program can simultaneously process samples from multiple fluorescence images and output their target concentrations, achieving rapid and straightforward high-throughput detection. The platform accurately, rapidly and with high throughput detected iron in serum. Additionally, we also measured the concentration of iron ions in drinking water, and the results were accurate. This demonstrates that the portable platform has widespread applicability.

The hydrophobic CQDs synthesized in this study have a fluorescence yield of 36.6% and stable photoluminescence, exhibiting high selectivity for iron. Through the integration of CQDs with HDESs, the sample was able to concentrate iron ions during the treatment process while simultaneously achieving detection. The synergistic use of the two materials enhanced the accuracy and sensitivity of the detection process. The limit of detection reached 33 nM, approaching the lowest detection limit of ICP-MS. Furthermore, DFT calculations showed that CQDs possess a stable rigid planar structure, promoting the emission of stable fluorescence signals. It was also determined that the components of the synthesized HDESs are connected by hydrogen bonds. The machine learning-assisted portable platform could capture fluorescence images of samples directly. These images are processed through an application developed with the YOLOv8 algorithm. This application efficiently analyzes multiple samples contained within single or multiple images and concurrently extracts color data from each sample. It then outputs the actual concentration of iron in multiple samples, enabling fast and reliable detection of the substance. The RSD was less than 10% for both the single and multiple sample tests. The design flexibility of HDES enhances the application value of this detection platform. By adjusting the types and ratios of hydrogen bond acceptors and donors, we can tailor specific detection platforms to the properties of target compounds. However, there are certain limitations in this work, such as colored substances in samples with inherently darker colors may affect the detection results when applying this method. Therefore, pre-treatment of the sample is required before detection. This study developed a machine learning-assisted portable platform that provides a reliable method for high-throughput point-of-care testing (HT-POCT) of trace iron ions in serum. Furthermore, the platform demonstrates significant potential in clinical rapid testing, particularly in scenarios requiring the quick analysis of large sample volumes, where its efficiency and accuracy can greatly streamline the testing process. Additionally, the platform holds substantial theoretical and research value for the HT-POCT of other compounds, offering a versatile tool for broad applications in various biomedical and environmental testing settings.

Hui Li: Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft. Hengmao Yue: Software, Visualization, Writing – ML-related original draft. Haixiang Li: Data curation. Maolin Zhu: Resources. Xicheng He: Visualization. Meng Liu: Conceptualization. Xiaoxia Li: Investigation. Feng Qiu: Supervision.
